title: When and How to Disqualify a Supplier
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: A practical QP guide to supplier disqualification: when to trigger it under ICH Q9 and Q10, how to do it without disrupting supply, and the path back.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner in a cool teal and cyan palette over deep navy, with a subtle green accent. A gloved quality professional in a pristine pharmaceutical facility places a red quarantine hold label on a sealed material container on a polished stainless-steel bench, while a softly blurred tablet displays a supplier status dashboard and rows of glass vials recede out of focus behind. Soft natural light from a side window, shallow depth of field with crisp focus on the hands and the container, calm, precise and decisive mood, 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos.

faqs.q: What is the difference between suspending and disqualifying a supplier?
faqs.a: Suspension is a temporary hold on new orders while a serious issue is investigated, and it is reversible if the supplier remediates effectively. Disqualification is the permanent removal of a supplier from your approved supplier list because confidence cannot reasonably be restored. Suspension is often the immediate protective step taken while you gather the evidence and risk assessment that may or may not lead to full disqualification.

faqs.q: Can a disqualified supplier ever be re-approved?
faqs.a: Yes, where the original cause was remediable rather than fraudulent. Re-approval should be treated as a fresh, documented qualification decision supported by independently verified CAPA, a successful follow-up audit and a sustained period of acceptable performance. Confirmed data integrity fraud, by contrast, will usually prevent re-approval permanently because it undermines trust in everything the supplier reports.

faqs.q: How do you disqualify a sole-source critical supplier without causing a shortage?
faqs.a: Make the quality decision and the supply-continuity decision together rather than trading one against the other. Suspend ordering immediately if patient safety is at risk, but sequence the formal exit so a qualified alternative source is in place first, and assess whether the MHRA needs to be notified of a potential shortage. The affected stock already held must also be risk-assessed, quarantined where necessary, and dispositioned before the transition is complete.
